title: [Bug]: Webchat dark theme secondary text (--muted) fails WCAG AA contrast (3.1-3.5:1) — readability regression, no contrast guardrail
author: woohahahaaa
body:
**Bug type:** Behavior bug (incorrect output/state without crash) — accessibility / readability regression
**Beta release blocker:** No

## Summary

The webchat UI's secondary/tertiary text color (`--muted`) in **dark theme** falls below the WCAG AA contrast minimum (4.5:1 for normal text), making secondary text hard to read. This is a dark-theme-specific regression; the light theme with the same token name passes. There is no automated contrast check, so palette changes can silently regress readability.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Open webchat on `2026.7.2` (or `main`) and select the **dark** theme.
2. Look at any UI text rendered with the `--muted` token (timestamps, secondary labels, hints, disabled/placeholder text, footer/metadata).
3. Measure the foreground/background contrast (computed from the shipped `base.css` tokens below).

## Expected behavior

All text meets WCAG AA:
- Normal text ≥ 4.5:1
- Large text ≥ 3.0:1

The dark theme should not ship secondary text that is dimmer than the AA floor on its actual backgrounds.

## Actual behavior

Computed contrast from the dark theme tokens in `ui/src/styles/base.css`:

| Foreground | Background | Contrast | AA (4.5) |
|---|---|---|---|
| `--muted` `#6e6960` | `--bg` `#0e1015` | **3.49** | ❌ FAIL |
| `--muted` `#6e6960` | `--bg-elevated` `#191c24` | **3.12** | ❌ FAIL |
| `--muted` `#6e6960` | `--bg-muted` `#1f2330` | **2.87** | ❌ FAIL |

For reference, the same `--muted` token in **light** theme passes (5.18:1 on `#faf9f7`). So this is a dark-theme-specific regression where `--muted` was dimmed but never re-validated against dark backgrounds.

Primary text (`--text` `#d4d4d8`) is fine at 12.87:1; the problem is specifically the secondary/muted color used for the most readability-sensitive small text.

## OpenClaw version

`2026.7.2` (target of latest auto-update; `main` branch `base.css` at time of filing)

## Operating system

macOS 15.7.7 (x64) — but this is a CSS color-token issue, OS-independent.

## Install method

npm global

## Model

N/A — pure UI / styling issue, no model involved.

## Provider / routing chain

N/A — pure UI / styling issue.

## Logs

Relevant shipped tokens (`ui/src/styles/base.css`, dark theme block):

```css
--bg: #0e1015;
--bg-elevated: #191c24;
--bg-muted: #1f2330;
--text: #d4d4d8;
--text-strong: #f4f4f5;
--muted: #6e6960;   /* secondary text */
```

Contrast computation (sRGB relative luminance → WCAG ratio):

```
--muted #6e6960 on --bg #0e1015        = 3.49:1  FAIL (AA needs 4.5)
--muted #6e6960 on --bg-elevated #191c24 = 3.12:1  FAIL
--muted #6e6960 on --bg-muted #1f2330    = 2.87:1  FAIL
--text #d4d4d8 on --bg #0e1015          = 12.87:1 PASS
light: --muted #6e6960 on #faf9f7        = 5.18:1  PASS (reference)
```

A comment already present in `base.css` shows the author believes `--muted #6e6960 ≈ 5.2:1 AA ✓` — but that figure is for the **light** theme. The same hex on dark backgrounds drops to ~3.1–3.5:1, so the "verified" contrast does not hold for dark mode.

## Impact and severity

- **Affected:** All webchat users on the dark theme (default for many), especially anyone relying on secondary text (timestamps, hints, metadata, disabled states).
- **Severity:** Medium — not a crash, but directly hurts readability/accessibility; users with low vision or in bright environments are worst hit.
- **Frequency:** Always reproducible on dark theme.
- **Consequence:** Secondary information is visually muddy / hard to read; the project ships an accessibility (WCAG AA) violation in its default-ish theme with no guardrail.

## Additional information

Suggested fixes:
1. Bump `--muted` (dark theme) to a lighter gray so secondary text ≥ 4.5:1 on `--bg`, `--bg-elevated`, and `--bg-muted` (e.g. roughly `#8a857c`–`#94908a` range — confirm with a check).
2. Add an automated contrast test (e.g. a unit test over the theme token map) that asserts every text token meets AA on every background it is used on, in **both** light and dark. This prevents future silent regressions like the one where light-theme contrast was assumed to apply to dark.
3. Consider exposing the computed contrast in a `/status` or dev overlay so regressions are visible in CI.

title: [Bug]: Control UI user avatar selector silently does nothing in 2026.7.1
author: delimir
body:
### Bug type

Regression (worked before, now fails)

### Beta release blocker

No

### Summary

In OpenClaw 2026.7.1, selecting or clearing the browser-local User avatar in Settings → Simple silently does nothing because the avatar does not update or persist.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Start OpenClaw 2026.7.1 and open the Control UI.
2. Go to Settings → Simple.
3. Under User avatar, click Choose image and select a valid image.
4. Observe that the avatar does not update.
5. Reload the Control UI and confirm that no avatar was persisted.
6. Click Clear avatar or enter an emoji/text avatar and observe the same silent no-op behavior.

### Expected behavior

The selected User avatar should update immediately and persist in browser-local identity storage, as documented in docs/web/control-ui.md.

This behavior worked in OpenClaw 2026.6.11, where the Quick Settings renderer passed `userAvatar` and `onUserAvatarChange` to the avatar editor.

### Actual behavior

Selecting, clearing, or changing the User avatar silently does nothing. No user-visible error is shown and the avatar is not persisted.

In the shipped 2026.7.1 Control UI bundle, the avatar editor still calls `onUserAvatarChange?.(...)`, but `renderQuickConfig()` does not pass `userAvatar` or `onUserAvatarChange`.

A local patch restoring browser-local avatar read/write wiring makes the selector work immediately.

### OpenClaw version

2026.7.1 (2d2ddc4)

### Operating system

macOS 26.5.2

### Install method

npm global

### Model

N/A — Control UI browser-local avatar regression

### Provider / routing chain

N/A — reproduced in Control UI before any model request

### Additional provider/model setup details

N/A — issue is independent of model/provider configuration.

### Logs

```shell
No gateway error is emitted when selecting or clearing the User avatar; the UI action silently no-ops.

2026.6.11 Control UI Quick Settings wiring:

userAvatar:e.userAvatar??null,
onUserAvatarChange:t=>e.applyLocalUserIdentity?.({avatar:t}),

2026.7.1 renderQuickConfig() omits both userAvatar and onUserAvatarChange.

The 2026.7.1 avatar editor still invokes:

e.onUserAvatarChange?.(...)

A local patch restoring browser-local avatar read/write wiring immediately restores avatar selection and persistence.
```

### Screenshots, recordings, and evidence

Observed directly in OpenClaw 2026.7.1 Control UI.

Regression comparison was made against the shipped npm bundles for 2026.6.11 and 2026.7.1.

After locally restoring the missing user avatar state/change wiring in the 2026.7.1 Quick Settings parent, selecting an avatar works and persists after reload.

### Impact and severity

_No response_

### Additional information

_No response_

title: [Bug]: Auto-updater applies a version requiring a newer Node with no engine check, no warning, and no user prompt — leaves the install broken
author: woohahahaaa
body:
**Bug type:** Crash (process/app exits or hangs) — triggered by an auto-update
**Beta release blocker:** No

## Summary

OpenClaw's auto-updater applies a newer version whose `engines.node` requirement is NOT satisfied by the currently installed Node, without any pre-check, warning, or rollback. The install is left broken and the failure gives no Node-version hint. Worse, the update is not user-initiated, so the user never gets a chance to upgrade Node first.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Have an environment where OpenClaw is installed globally via npm and the active `node` is below the new version's engine floor (see evidence: `v22.22.2` vs required `>=22.22.3`).
2. Let OpenClaw auto-update to a newer release (this is not triggered by a manual `openclaw update` / user click — it happens on its own).
3. After the update completes, OpenClaw fails to start / crashes.
4. Inspect: the failure is caused by the Node engine requirement of the new version not being met, but no message at the point of failure tells the user "you need a newer Node".

## Expected behavior

The auto-updater should:
- Pre-check `engines.node` of the target version against the running Node **before** applying the update.
- If the new version requires a newer Node, **warn the user** and either skip the update or present a clear, actionable path (e.g. "OpenClaw X requires Node >=22.22.3, but you have 22.22.2. Upgrade Node first, or this update will be skipped").
- Never leave the install in a broken state (i.e. no silent upgrade into an unrunnable build).

## Actual behavior

The update proceeded. After it applied, OpenClaw failed (the new build cannot run on the installed Node). There was no upfront engine check and no clear "Node version too old" message at the failure point. Because the update was automatic and not user-initiated, the user had no opportunity to prepare the runtime beforehand.

## OpenClaw version

`2026.7.1` (installed) → auto-update target `2026.7.2`

## Operating system

macOS 15.7.7 (x64)

## Install method

npm global

## Model

N/A — failure occurs at upgrade/boot before a model is selected.

## Provider / routing chain

N/A — failure occurs at upgrade/boot stage.

## Logs

Verified precondition reproduced in this environment (semver check):

```
$ node -e '...'
current node: 22.22.2
required   : >=22.22.3 <23 || >=24.15.0 <25 || >=25.9.0
satisfies  : NO  <-- old node does not meet new version requirement
```

Target version engine requirement (`package.json` of `2026.7.2` on `main`):
```json
"engines": {
  "node": ">=22.22.3 <23 || >=24.15.0 <25 || >=25.9.0"
}
```

Local versions present:
```
$ node -v
v22.22.2                # active default — BELOW the new floor
$ ls /usr/local/n/versions/node
22.22.2
# A compatible v22.22.3 binary exists at /Users/Agent/local/node-v22.22.3-darwin-x64/bin/node
# but is not the default `node` on PATH, so the auto-updated build cannot run.
```

Installed vs latest:
```
local openclaw package.json version: 2026.7.1
github.com/openclaw/openclaw latest (main package.json): 2026.7.2
```

## Impact and severity

- **Affected:** Any user on an auto-update channel whose installed Node is below the new release's `engines.node` floor.
- **Severity:** High — OpenClaw becomes unusable after the auto-update until the user (a) figures out the cause and (b) manually upgrades Node.
- **Frequency:** Reproducible whenever a release bumps the Node minimum and the host Node is just under it. The `22.22.2` → `>=22.22.3` bump is a near-miss that hits this exact edge.
- **Consequence:** Silent breakage of a running gateway/agent with no actionable error; especially bad because the user never asked for the update.

## Additional information

- The new version's Node floor (`>=22.22.3`) is a **patch-level** bump over the previously-working `22.22.2`, which makes the failure surprising and easy to miss in logs.
- Suggested fix: gate the auto-updater on `engines.node` compatibility (use the same npm `engines` semantics), and if incompatible, emit an explicit, blocking warning + recovery instruction instead of applying a broken build. Optionally ship a Node-version check on startup that prints the exact mismatch.
- A compatible Node (`v22.22.3`) is already available on the host but not on `PATH`; the updater could also detect/offer this.

title: [Feature]: Upload files into the Control UI terminal
author: steipete
body:
## Summary

Let operators drag files into the Control UI terminal, or choose one or more files with an upload button, and receive usable paths in the active shell.

## Problem to solve

The browser terminal can send keyboard input but cannot transfer a local file to the machine that owns the PTY. Operators currently need a separate transfer tool, then manually locate and quote the remote path. This interrupts common image, PDF, log, and archive workflows and is especially awkward for paired-node terminals.

## Proposed solution

- Accept file drag-and-drop on the active terminal viewport.
- Add a small file-picker action with single- and multi-select support.
- Stage each file on the machine that owns the terminal: the Gateway for local terminals, or the paired node for node-backed terminals.
- Paste shell-safe absolute paths into the active terminal using the terminal's native paste path. Do not press Enter or execute anything.
- Support arbitrary file types, with a 16 MiB limit per file and clear inline errors.
- Store uploads as mode-0600 temporary user artifacts under a private OpenClaw upload directory, with bounded cleanup.
- Keep the existing `operator.admin` terminal authorization and session ownership checks. A terminal session may upload only through its bound host.

## Alternatives considered

- The bundled file-transfer plugin has useful size and encoding patterns, but its agent-tool policy is deny-by-default and it is optional. It is not the right owner for an operator-admin terminal transport.
- Saving directly into the shell's current working directory is surprising after `cd`, can overwrite user files, and is ambiguous for remote PTYs. A temporary absolute path is safer and still easy to move.
- Clipboard-only upload cannot represent arbitrary binary files reliably and does not solve paired-node transfer.

## Impact

Affected: Control UI operators using local or paired-node terminals.

Severity: Medium; blocks direct file-driven terminal workflows.

Frequency: Common when inspecting images, PDFs, logs, archives, and generated artifacts.

Consequence: Extra transfer tooling, context switching, and path/quoting mistakes.

## Evidence/examples

Terminal applications commonly turn dragged files into shell-quoted paths. The browser adds one missing step: transferring bytes to the PTY host first. No open issue or PR was found for terminal drag-and-drop, terminal file upload, or paired-node terminal transfer.

## Additional information

Implementation should cover protocol schemas, local and paired-node backends, Control UI keyboard/accessibility behavior, focused unit tests, mocked-browser E2E, docs, and before/after screenshots.

title: [Bug]: memsearch-summarize sub-session compaction fails with "No API provider registered for api: ollama" on 2026.7.1, while main-session compaction with the same provider succeeds
author: hypothesis-next
body:
# [Bug]: memsearch-summarize sub-session compaction fails with "No API provider registered for api: ollama" on 2026.7.1, while main-session compaction with the same provider succeeds

### Environment
- **OpenClaw**: 2026.7.1 (2d2ddc4), npm global install
- **Node**: v24.18.0
- **OS**: macOS 26.5.2, arm64 (Apple Silicon)
- **Provider**: native Ollama, local, running qwen3.6

### Summary
On 2026.7.1, a natively-configured Ollama provider (`api: "ollama"`) works for normal generation **and** for the main agent session's auto-compaction, but the **memsearch-summarize** sub-session's auto-compaction consistently fails with:

```
auto-compaction failed for ollama/qwen3.6: No API provider registered for api: ollama
```

This is the same error family as the (closed) #57099 / #25669, but it is **not** fully fixed: the main compaction path was repaired, while the summarize sub-session path still hits the unregistered-provider error. This looks related to #66620 (compaction-summary path degrading to truncation) but manifests as the hard "No API provider registered" throw rather than empty output.

### Provider config (`openclaw.json`)
```json
{
  "api": "ollama",
  "baseUrl": "http://localhost:11434",
  "models": [
    {
      "api": "ollama",
      "contextWindow": 20480,
      "id": "qwen3.6",
      "name": "qwen3.6",
      "params": { "num_ctx": 20480 }
    }
  ]
}
```
`agents.defaults.compaction`: `{ reserveTokens: 3000, reserveTokensFloor: 3000, keepRecentTokens: 4000 }`

### The key diagnostic (same provider, same route, different outcome)
Two sessions overflow within the same minute, both `provider=ollama/qwen3.6`, both `route=compact_only`:

- **Main user session** — compaction **succeeds**:
  ```
  sessionKey=agent:main:openresponses-user:<user>  provider=ollama/qwen3.6  route=compact_only
  auto-compaction succeeded for ollama/qwen3.6; retrying prompt
  ```
- **memsearch-summarize sub-session** — compaction **fails**:
  ```
  sessionKey=agent:main:explicit:memsearch-summarize  provider=ollama/qwen3.6  route=compact_only
  [context-overflow-precheck] estimatedPromptTokens=19256 promptBudgetBeforeReserve=17480 overflowTokens=1776 route=compact_only
  context overflow detected (attempt 1/3); attempting auto-compaction for ollama/qwen3.6
  auto-compaction failed for ollama/qwen3.6: No API provider registered for api: ollama
  Context overflow: prompt too large for the model. Try /reset (or /new) to start a fresh session, or use a larger-context model.
  [compaction-diag] ... trigger=overflow attempt=1 maxAttempts=3 outcome=failed reason=unknown detail=No_API_provider_registered_for_api:_ollama
  ```

So the provider registry is populated for the main agent's compaction context but the `explicit:memsearch-summarize` session's compaction resolves against a context where `api: "ollama"` is not registered.

### Impact
- Bounded and non-fatal: single attempt, no retry loop; falls back to truncation; the user-facing turn still completes (the "try /reset" line is emitted inside the background summarize session and is not delivered to the channel).
- But memory summaries are **truncated rather than compacted** on every overflowing summarize turn (~2×/turn here), degrading the quality of captured memory over time.

### Repro
1. Configure a native local Ollama provider (`api: "ollama"`) as above; enable the memsearch plugin with `autoCapture: true`.
2. Hold a conversation long enough that the per-turn memsearch-summarize session exceeds its token budget (~19k vs a ~17.5k budget here).
3. Observe the summarize session's auto-compaction throw "No API provider registered for api: ollama" while the main session's compaction (when it overflows) succeeds.

### Expected
The `explicit:memsearch-summarize` (and other internal/sub-agent) compaction paths should resolve the same provider registry as the main agent path, so a natively-configured Ollama provider can compact summarize sessions instead of falling back to truncation.

### Related
#57099, #25669 (closed — main/`/compact` path, older versions), #66620 (compaction-summary path, Ollama cloud, empty output), #69683 (pi-ai builtin registry lacks `ollama`).

title: Feature: option to suppress foreground exec non-zero exit notifications
author: riderrzn
body:
## Context

When a foreground `exec` call returns a non-zero exit code, OpenClaw injects a system event notification (`⚠️ 🛠️ Exec failed: ...`) into the chat. This happens even when the non-zero exit is **expected and benign** — for example:

- `grep` with no matches (exit 1)
- `systemctl stop` on an already-removed unit (exit 5)
- `systemctl --user stop` on a system-level unit (exit 1)

These are not real failures — they are normal shell behavior. But the notification noise makes it look like something went wrong, confusing the user.

## Current behavior

- `tools.exec.notifyOnExit` only applies to **backgrounded** exec sessions
- `tools.exec.notifyOnExitEmptySuccess` also background-only
- There is **no config option** to suppress or tune non-zero exit notifications for **foreground** exec calls

## Proposed solution

Add a config option, e.g.:

```json5
{
  tools: {
    exec: {
      notifyOnNonZeroExit: false  // or "background-only" | true (default)
    }
  }
}
```

- `true` (default): current behavior — notify on any non-zero exit
- `false`: never notify on foreground non-zero exits
- `"background-only"`: only notify for backgrounded sessions (current `notifyOnExit` behavior), suppress foreground

## Workaround

Agents can append `|| true` to commands that are expected to return non-zero, but this is fragile and easy to forget. A config-level toggle would be cleaner.

## Environment

- OpenClaw 2026.7.1
- Linux x64, Node v24.18.0
- Telegram channel

title: [Bug]: exec tool fires ⚠️ failure notification for benign non-zero exits (grep exit 1, git rebase conflict)
author: JeffSteinbok
body:
**Describe the bug**

Commands that exit with code 1 for non-failure reasons (e.g. `grep` finding no matches, `git` rebase exiting with a conflict) trigger the `⚠️ 🛠️ Exec failed: `...`` Discord notification. This produces spurious noise for normal agent operation.

**Reproduction**

Run any of the following in an agent session connected to a Discord channel:

```bash
grep "nonexistent-pattern" ~/some-file  # exits 1 (no match)
git pull --rebase                        # exits 1 if conflict
```

Expected: no notification (these are benign non-zero exits that the agent handles normally).
Actual: `⚠️ 🛠️ Exec failed: `<command description>`` is posted to the channel.

**Root cause**

`src/agents/tool-result-error.ts` → `isToolResultError()` classifies any `exitCode !== 0` in tool result details as an error:

```ts
const exitCode = details ? readToolErrorField(details, "exitCode") : undefined;
return typeof exitCode === "number" && Number.isFinite(exitCode) && exitCode !== 0;
```

The exec runtime in `bash-tools.exec-runtime.ts` already correctly distinguishes clean non-zero exits (`status: "completed"`) from real failures (`status: "failed"`). A `grep` with no results exits cleanly with `reason: "exit"`, code `1` → `status: "completed"`. But the tool result error classifier doesn't check `status` — it only checks `exitCode`, overriding the runtime's own determination.

**Proposed fix**

In `isToolResultError()`, skip the `exitCode !== 0` check when the result's `status` is `"completed"`:

```ts
const exitCode = details ? readToolErrorField(details, "exitCode") : undefined;
if (typeof exitCode !== "number" || !Number.isFinite(exitCode) || exitCode === 0) return false;
const status = readToolResultStatus(result);
if (status === "completed") return false;
return true;
```

This correctly defers to the exec runtime's own classification rather than second-guessing it from the exit code alone.

**Additional context**

- Affected tool: `exec` (and `bash`)
- Notification format: `⚠️ 🛠️ Exec failed: `<command description>``
- Source function: `formatToolErrorWarningText()` in `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/payloads.ts`
- The notification fires when `isToolResultError()` → `observerIsError = true` → `isToolError = true` → `lastToolError` is set → `showWarning: !hasUserFacingReply`
- Workaround: append `|| true` to commands that may exit 1 for non-failure reasons

title: [Bug]: Codex quota recovery/reset credits do not re-probe long WHAM cooldowns when fallbacks exist
author: ndj888
body:
### Bug type

Behavior bug (incorrect output/state without crash)

### Beta release blocker

No

### Summary

After a real `openai/gpt-5.5` subscription-limit 429, OpenClaw 2026.6.5 persisted a six-day WHAM block and kept skipping the provider to a configured fallback even after the same account successfully ran Codex and reported 12% weekly usage with no reached limit.

### Steps to reproduce

This is a live observed sequence; account identifiers and tokens are redacted.

1. Install OpenClaw `2026.6.5` with npm global on Debian 13.
2. Configure `openai/gpt-5.5` as primary and `opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash` as fallback #1.
3. Run the primary until the upstream returns HTTP 429 with `You've reached your Codex subscription usage limit` and a reset time about six days away.
4. Observe that OpenClaw stores the OpenAI auth profile with `blockedReason=subscription_limit`, `blockedSource=wham`, and `blockedUntil=<the reported reset>`.
5. Send later requests. OpenClaw skips the primary with `Provider openai is in cooldown (suspending lanes)` and immediately succeeds on the DeepSeek fallback.
6. Later, use Codex CLI with the same email and account ID. A real `gpt-5.3-codex-spark` request succeeds, and `account/rateLimits` reports a 10,080-minute weekly window at `used_percent=12`, with `rate_limit_reached_type=null`.
7. Send another OpenClaw request. The stored block still causes the OpenAI provider to be skipped; it is not invalidated by the now-usable account state.

### Expected behavior

When the same subscription reports non-blocked quota or succeeds on another Codex model before the cached reset timestamp, OpenClaw should re-evaluate the preferred provider. With a configured fallback, recovery should still use a bounded re-probe or fresh WHAM/rate-limit check rather than trusting a multi-day `blockedUntil` until literal expiry.

A fresh non-blocked rate-limit snapshot, a reset-credit change, or a successful same-account Codex request should trigger a model/limit-scoped re-evaluation. If the originally blocked model still returns 429, the block can be re-armed; if it succeeds, the stale block should clear without manual state editing.

### Actual behavior

The first subscription-limit response becomes a long-lived provider-profile block. Because a fallback exists and succeeds, subsequent requests keep skipping `openai/gpt-5.5` without checking whether the subscription has become usable again.

The observed stored state remained:

```json
{
  "blockedUntil": 1784507779363,
  "blockedReason": "subscription_limit",
  "blockedSource": "wham",
  "errorCount": 1,
  "failureCounts": { "rate_limit": 1 }
}
```

Meanwhile, the same account completed a Codex request and returned a non-blocked weekly quota snapshot.

### OpenClaw version

2026.6.5 (5181e4f)

### Operating system

Debian GNU/Linux 13 (trixie), Node.js v22.22.1

### Install method

npm global

### Model

Primary: `openai/gpt-5.5`; successful same-account verification: `gpt-5.3-codex-spark`

### Provider / routing chain

`openai/gpt-5.5` -> `opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash`

### Additional provider/model setup details

- One OpenAI OAuth profile was active.
- OpenClaw and Codex CLI were verified to use the same redacted email and the same account ID.
- The OpenClaw OAuth credential was not expired.
- The DeepSeek fallback was healthy, so requests continued working but did not return to the preferred provider.

### Logs

```shell
2026-07-14T02:27:39.403Z auth_profile_failure_state_updated
provider=openai reason=rate_limit windowType=cooldown errorCount=1

2026-07-14T02:27:39.423Z embedded_run_failover_decision
provider=openai model=gpt-5.5 status=429
rawErrorPreview="You've reached your Codex subscription usage limit. Next reset in 6 days, Jul 19 at 8:36 PM EDT."

2026-07-14T06:56:55.097Z model_fallback_decision
decision=skip_candidate candidate=openai/gpt-5.5 reason=rate_limit
errorPreview="Provider openai is in cooldown (suspending lanes)"
nextCandidate=opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash

2026-07-14T06:57:05.655Z model_fallback_decision
decision=candidate_succeeded candidate=opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash

2026-07-14T07:19:22.664Z same-account Codex CLI token_count
model=gpt-5.3-codex-spark
rate_limits={
  "limit_id":"codex",
  "primary":{"used_percent":12.0,"window_minutes":10080,"resets_at":1784543650},
  "rate_limit_reached_type":null
}
```

### Screenshots, recordings, and evidence

No screenshot. The redacted gateway log timeline and stored auth-profile state above are the direct evidence.

### Impact and severity

- Affected: OAuth-backed Codex/OpenAI users with a usable fallback and a subscription block whose real availability changes before `blockedUntil`.
- Severity: medium to high; work continues, but on the wrong provider/model and may incur unintended quota or cost.
- Frequency: every request after the stored block, until literal expiry or manual intervention.
- Consequence: the preferred Codex/OpenAI route can remain untested for days even after account-level quota state changes.

### Additional information

This appears related to, but distinct from:

- #90702 / #90717: recovery probing when **no fallback** exists. That fix intentionally left multi-fallback behavior unchanged; this report has a healthy fallback.
- #99809 / #99810: scoping a WHAM subscription block to the failing model. This report additionally concerns invalidating a previously stored block when the same subscription becomes usable before the reported reset.
- #100556: provider-wide cooldown skipping healthy sibling profiles/models.

The live account response did not expose a specific reset-credit ID, so this report does not claim that a particular credit caused the recovery. It proves that the same account became usable while OpenClaw retained the old block. This matters more now that Codex can grant reset credits or otherwise update available quota before an earlier long reset timestamp.

Possible behavior directions, without assuming implementation details:

1. Give multi-day WHAM blocks a bounded re-probe interval even when a fallback is serving traffic.
2. Refresh `account/rateLimits`/WHAM state before honoring an old `subscription_limit` block after its snapshot becomes stale.
3. Invalidate or shorten `blockedUntil` when reset credits or a non-blocked snapshot is observed.
4. Preserve the provider-reported full-reset timestamp for display, but do not use it as an unconditional no-probe deadline.

title: [Bug]: External Control UI tab cannot render gateway-auth plugin route under token auth
author: paulpitchford
body:
## Summary

OpenClaw 2026.7.1 advertises an external plugin's `surface: "tab"` descriptor through Gateway hello and renders the sidebar item, but the tab's plain iframe request cannot authenticate a plugin HTTP route registered with `auth: "gateway"` when the Gateway uses token auth. The result is a visible plugin tab whose frame returns `401 Unauthorized`.

This leaves external plugins unable to use the documented `path` + `registerHttpRoute(...)` integration without either making the route public/plugin-managed or placing a credential in the iframe URL.

## Environment

- OpenClaw `2026.7.1 (2d2ddc4)`
- Gateway auth mode: `token`
- `gateway.auth.allowTailscale: true`
- Control UI accessed over HTTPS through Tailscale Serve
- External plugin loaded from a local link

## Minimal plugin shape

```ts
api.session.controls.registerControlUiDescriptor({
  id: "productivity-hub",
  surface: "tab",
  label: "Productivity Hub",
  icon: "barChart",
  group: "control",
  order: 50,
  path: "/productivity-hub",
});

api.registerHttpRoute({
  path: "/productivity-hub",
  match: "prefix",
  auth: "gateway",
  handler,
});
```

## Reproduction

1. Enable the plugin and restart the Gateway.
2. Open and authenticate the normal Control UI through Tailscale Serve.
3. Confirm Gateway hello includes the plugin in `controlUiTabs`.
4. Expand the Control navigation and select the plugin tab.
5. Inspect the iframe request.

## Actual result

- Gateway hello contains the expected descriptor, including `path: "/productivity-hub"`.
- The sidebar displays the plugin tab.
- Selecting it navigates the dashboard to `/plugin?plugin=productivity-hub&id=productivity-hub`.
- The iframe issues a plain `GET /productivity-hub` and receives `401`.
- The authenticated Control UI connection has operator read/write/admin scopes, but browser storage contains the connection/device state and there is no HTTP auth cookie.
- Direct unauthenticated `GET /productivity-hub` also returns `401`, which is the required security behavior.

The shipped plugin-page code sets `src` directly from the descriptor path. It does not provide an authenticated fetch/proxy/session handoff for the framed route.

## Expected result

The documented external-plugin tab path should be able to render a Gateway-authenticated plugin HTTP route from an already authenticated Control UI without:

- making the route public;
- changing it to plugin-managed auth;
- putting the Gateway token/password in the URL; or
- granting broader operator authority than required.

A path-scoped HttpOnly launch session, a host-mediated authenticated proxy, or an equivalent non-URL credential handoff would preserve the route boundary.

## Security note

Please do not solve this by weakening plugin route auth or serializing the shared Gateway credential into the iframe URL. The route must continue to return `401` for a direct unauthenticated request.

## Related context

- The Plugin SDK docs describe external tab `path` values as plugin HTTP routes rendered in sandboxed frames.
- The Gateway configuration docs state that Tailscale identity headers may satisfy Control UI/WebSocket auth, while HTTP endpoints continue to use normal Gateway HTTP auth.
- Earlier discussions/proposals: #66944, #71736, and #80388.

title: [Bug]: Telegram durable ingress loses messages after restart: PID reused as thread ID fakes a live claim owner, then global update-ID watermark rejects the delayed replays
author: obviyus
body:
### Bug type

Behavior bug (incorrect output/state without crash)

### Beta release blocker

No

### Summary

After a gateway restart, two Telegram group messages were durably spooled but never dispatched to the model: a stale ingress claim survived because the dead owner's PID was reused as a Linux *thread* ID inside the new gateway process (so `processExists()` reported it alive), and once the lane was unwedged the delayed updates were rejected as `accepted-watermark` duplicates because other lanes had already advanced the global update-ID watermark past them.

### Steps to reproduce

Two independent mechanisms, both reproduced from the incident evidence:

**A. Stale claim survives restart via PID/TID reuse**
1. Polling worker claims spooled update U in lane L (claim owner `${pid}:${uuid}`, e.g. PID 9).
2. Gateway receives SIGTERM after the turn is durably adopted but before ingress completion is persisted; process restarts (new PID 8).
3. In the restarted Node process, numeric ID 9 exists again as a *thread* of PID 8 (`/proc/9/status`: `Name: DelayedTaskSche`, `Tgid: 8`, `Pid: 9`).
4. `isTelegramSpooledUpdateClaimOwnedByOtherLiveProcess()` (`extensions/telegram/src/telegram-ingress-spool.ts:225`) sees `processPid !== process.pid` and `processExists(9)` → treats the dead owner as live; stale-claim recovery (`extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.ts:945`) refuses to reclaim for the full 30-minute lease. Every later update in lane L stays `pending` (attempts=0, claim_token=null).

**B. Delayed replay rejected by global update-ID watermark**
1. Telegram update IDs are global across chats. While lane L is wedged behind the claim, lanes for other chats process newer update IDs, advancing `highestAcceptedUpdateId` in `createTelegramUpdateTracker()`.
2. When lane L's spooled updates finally replay, their IDs are below the global watermark and are rejected as `accepted-watermark` (`extensions/telegram/src/bot-update-tracker.ts:187-196`).
3. The floor-replay exception only covers IDs at or below the persistence floor captured at Bot creation (`extensions/telegram/src/bot-update-tracker.ts:134-137`, `extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.ts:510-516`); updates that arrived after that floor but got delayed in one lane are not covered. Their queue rows flip to `completed` with zero model dispatch — silent message loss.

### Expected behavior

Durably spooled updates are dispatched exactly once even across a restart: a claim whose owner process is dead must be recoverable (a thread ID of the current process is not a live foreign owner), and a legitimately delayed cross-lane replay must not be swallowed by a watermark advanced by other chats.

### Actual behavior

Incident on lane `telegram:-1003193947289` (2026-07-14 UTC):

- 06:33:06 update 432832292 ("ty boss", msg 51084) claimed by owner `9:9dcc4076-…`.
- 06:33:15 gateway SIGTERM + restart (new PID 8). Startup recovery adopted and completed the orphaned turn (06:33:45, `NO_REPLY`) — but the ingress row stayed `claimed`.
- `/proc/9` existed as a thread of PID 8 → claim judged live-owned → lane wedged under the 30-min lease.
- Updates 432832296 and 432832297 (two direct owner requests, msgs 51086/51087) stayed `pending`, attempts=0, no claim, no error. Other chats kept working.
- After the completed blocker was manually tombstoned, the lane drained — but both updates were rejected as `accepted-watermark` duplicates (newer IDs had completed in other lanes) and their rows became `completed` without ever reaching the message cache, dedupe state, session transcript, or any model dispatch. Both user messages were permanently lost.

### OpenClaw version

2026.7.2-dev, main @ `6b0a835116a` (all cited code identical at current main `e91d0fc8b7e`)

### Operating system

Linux (Debian-based Docker container; Linux home-lab host)

### Install method

docker compose (source build)

### Model

claude-fable-5 via claude-cli backend — not reached; failure is pre-dispatch

### Provider / routing chain

telegram long-poll → durable ingress spool (SQLite queue `["telegram","default"]`) → embedded agent (dispatch never began)

### Additional provider/model setup details

Isolated ingress enabled for polling (`initialUpdateId: null` + fixed persistence floor, `extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.ts:510-516`).

### Logs

```
Blocking queue row (state/openclaw.sqlite):
  event 0000000432832292  update_id 432832292  msg 51084  lane telegram:-1003193947289
  status claimed  claimed_at 1784010786378 (2026-07-14T06:33:06.378Z)
  claim_owner 9:9dcc4076-265f-40a3-8487-30e7825dc18e

Lost rows (post-drain: completed, but zero dispatch artifacts):
  event 0000000432832296  update_id 432832296  msg 51086  attempts 0 -> completed, no model dispatch
  event 0000000432832297  update_id 432832297  msg 51087  attempts 0 -> completed, no model dispatch

Restarted process:
  gateway PID 8
  /proc/9/status: Name: DelayedTaskSche  Tgid: 8  Pid: 9   <- old claim-owner PID now a thread of the new process

Timeline:
  06:33:06 update 432832292 claimed
  06:33:15 SIGTERM, restart
  06:33:37 restart recovery resubmitted the orphaned turn
  06:33:45 recovered run completed (NO_REPLY); ingress row still `claimed`
```

### Screenshots, recordings, and evidence

Code paths:
- 30-min lease + `${process.pid}:${randomUUID()}` owner identity: `extensions/telegram/src/telegram-ingress-spool.ts:32-38`
- foreign-live-owner check (PID inequality + `processExists(pid)`): `extensions/telegram/src/telegram-ingress-spool.ts:225-236`
- recovery refuses "live-owned" claims: `extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.ts:945-959`
- global `highestAcceptedUpdateId` + startup-only floor replay: `extensions/telegram/src/bot-update-tracker.ts:127-137`
- `accepted-watermark` rejection: `extensions/telegram/src/bot-update-tracker.ts:183-199`
- tracker initialization: `extensions/telegram/src/bot-core.ts:162-176`

Existing test `extensions/telegram/src/telegram-ingress-spool.test.ts` ("does not treat stale claims with reused pids as live-owned") only covers sufficiently *old* claims — not a fresh claim whose PID number now belongs to a thread (TID) of the current process.

### Impact and severity

- Affected: any Telegram deployment using the durable ingress spool; any restart while a claim is held can wedge that chat's lane for up to 30 minutes, and any cross-lane delay can silently drop messages.
- Severity: high — silent, unrecoverable inbound message loss (rows marked `completed`, so no retry, no error, no operator signal).
- Frequency: intermittent (needs restart-during-claim for A; any cross-lane out-of-order replay for B — B can strike without A).
- Consequence: user messages never reach the agent; observed 2/2 delayed owner messages lost in this incident.

### Additional information

- The two bugs compound but are independent: B (watermark loss) can drop any spool replay that got delayed in one lane while other lanes advanced, even without A.
- Live repair already performed on the affected deployment: the completed blocker row was transactionally tombstoned using its exact claim token; the lane drained, new messages flow. The two lost messages had to be re-sent by the user.
- Restart recovery successfully completed the adopted turn but has no linkage tombstoning the corresponding spool claim — worth fixing alongside liveness detection so a recovered turn releases its claim explicitly.
- Fix PR (both mechanisms + regression tests) to follow.

title: WhatsApp group messages never reach inbound handling (LID groups?) — DMs work; repro across relink + 2026.6.11→2026.7.1
author: kaluyckx
body:
## Summary

WhatsApp group messages never reach OpenClaw's inbound handling — `gateway.call health` never updates `lastInboundAt`/`messagesHandled` for group traffic, and `directory groups list --channel whatsapp` always returns `[]` — even though WhatsApp's own delivery receipts confirm the message reached the linked device. Direct messages (DMs) on the same account work perfectly and instantly. This reproduces across a fresh QR relink and across a full gateway + plugin version update.

## Environment

- OpenClaw gateway: upgraded during triage from 2026.6.11 → **2026.7.1**
- `@openclaw/whatsapp` plugin: upgraded from 2026.6.11 → **2026.7.1** (via `plugins install @openclaw/whatsapp --force`)
- Deployment: self-hosted, Docker Compose, prebuilt GHCR image
- WhatsApp account type: WhatsApp Business, linked via QR (companion/multi-device session, not the Cloud API)
- Multi-agent setup: a second agent (`household`) routed via a top-level `bindings[]` entry: `{type: "route", agentId: "household", match: {channel: "whatsapp", accountId: "ferdinand"}}`

## Steps to reproduce

1. Link a WhatsApp Business account via `openclaw channels login --channel whatsapp --account <id>` (QR).
2. Confirm DM works: message the linked number directly — pairs via `dmPolicy: pairing`, replies correctly. `gateway call health` shows `lastInboundAt` populated, `messagesHandled` incrementing.
3. Add the linked number to a WhatsApp group as a normal member.
4. Send a message in that group.
5. Check `openclaw directory groups list --channel whatsapp --account <id> --limit 50 --json` → returns `[]`.
6. Check `gateway call health --json` for that account → `lastInboundAt: null`, unchanged.
7. On the sending phone, check the message's delivery info for the linked number specifically → shows delivered / read (double-grey then blue ticks), confirming WhatsApp did deliver it to that device.

## What was ruled out

- **`groupPolicy` setting**: tested all three values (`disabled`, `allowlist` with empty `groupAllowFrom`, and `open`/no filter at all). Same null result in every case — including fully `open`, which should bypass all filtering.
- **Stale/interrupted session state**: fully logged out (`channels logout`) and relinked from scratch via a fresh QR pairing. Same result on the new session.
- **Plugin/gateway version**: reproduced on both `2026.6.11` and, after updating, `2026.7.1` (gateway core) with the plugin explicitly reinstalled at `2026.7.1` too.
- **Config/binding mistake**: the account-level binding routes correctly for DMs (same account, same binding path), so the routing config itself is not the issue — it's specifically group-type messages that never arrive at the inbound handler.

## Hypothesis (unconfirmed)

WhatsApp's newer privacy feature addresses group participants via an LID (`@lid`) identity rather than the plain phone-number JID (`@s.whatsapp.net`) used for DMs. This account's self-identity has both (from `gateway call health`): `32456872514@s.whatsapp.net` and LID `33986831237279@lid`. Given DMs (JID-addressed) work perfectly and only group messages (which involve LID-based participant addressing) silently fail to register, a parsing/routing gap specific to LID-addressed group messages seems like the most likely root cause — though this wasn't confirmed against the plugin's source directly.

## Impact

WhatsApp group support is effectively non-functional for this setup — the provider reports healthy/connected the whole time, so there's no error surfaced anywhere; it just silently never sees group traffic.

title: `openclaw update` trusts `npm root -g` stdout verbatim, but npm >= 11 redacts UUID-like path segments to literal `***` — update then installs into a literal `***/` directory tree and silently leaves the live install stale
author: SL4N
body:
## Summary

`resolveGlobalRoot()` shells out to `npm root -g` and uses `res.stdout.trim()` as a real filesystem path. Since npm added `@npmcli/redact`, **all** npm output — including plain stdout of `npm root -g` / `npm prefix -g` — is passed through `redactLog`, which replaces every UUID-shaped substring (`[0-9a-f]{8}-[0-9a-f]{4}-...`) with the literal string `***`.

If the npm global prefix path contains a UUID-like segment (common for per-session sandboxes, ephemeral CI workspaces, tmpdir-scoped installs), `npm root -g` returns a **poisoned path** such as:

```
/tmp/ci-agent/***/workspace/sbx-***/nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/lib/node_modules
```

The updater then:

1. `fs.mkdir(globalRoot, { recursive: true })` — **creates a literal `***` directory tree on disk** (`createStagedNpmInstall`),
2. runs `npm i -g --prefix <***-path>/.openclaw-update-stage-XXXXXX <spec>` — installs the new version into the phantom tree,
3. swaps the staged package into `<***-path>/node_modules/openclaw`,
4. reports the update as installed, while the **real** global install is left untouched at the old version.

Net effect: `openclaw update` is a silent no-op for the live install, plus a junk parallel directory tree literally named `***`.

## Affected code (verified at v2026.6.11; still present on current `main`, commit `1b9aca26b`)

- `src/infra/update-global.ts` v2026.6.11 L737–743 (main L751–757): `resolveGlobalRoot` → `const root = res.stdout.trim(); return root || null;` — trusts redacted stdout.
- `src/infra/update-runner.ts` v2026.6.11 L1661: `resolveGlobalInstallTarget({ manager, runCommand, timeoutMs, pkgRoot })` — called **without** `honorPackageRoot`, so the (real, process-derived) `pkgRoot` never overrides the poisoned stdout value; `targetGlobalRoot` falls through to `globalRoot` (update-global.ts v2026.6.11 L782–786).
- `src/infra/package-update-steps.ts` v2026.6.11 L248–249: `createStagedNpmInstall` → `fs.mkdir(targetLayout.globalRoot, { recursive: true })` + `mkdtemp` — materializes the literal `***` tree; L499 (swap step) and L621 (`stagedInstall?.prefix` → `--prefix`) propagate it.
- npm side (npm 11.9.0, node 24.14.0): `node_modules/npm/lib/utils/format.js` — comment "All logging goes through here, both to console and log files" → `redactLog` from `@npmcli/redact` (`lib/index.js`: `const REPLACE = '***'` + `matchers.UUID.pattern`).

## Reproduction (fully scripted, no manual path handling)

Self-contained script (creates a UUID-named sandbox prefix, installs openclaw@2026.6.11, runs `openclaw update --yes --no-restart --json`, then does byte-level forensics via `os.listdir`/file reads — no shell display involved):

Observed on Linux arm64, node v24.14.0, npm 11.9.0:

- Mechanism probe (bytes captured via `subprocess`, not a terminal):
  `npm root -g` → `b'/tmp/ci-agent/***/workspace/sbx-***/nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/lib/node_modules\n'`
- Baseline: no `***` path exists anywhere.
- After `openclaw update`: a literal `***` tree exists (verified with byte-level `os.listdir`, entry `b'***'`), containing a freshly installed `openclaw@2026.7.1`; `update --json` step log shows
  `npm i -g --prefix /tmp/.../***/.../.openclaw-update-stage-AkrV8k openclaw@latest` (exit 0) and
  `swap .../***/.../.openclaw-update-stage-AkrV8k/lib/node_modules/openclaw -> .../***/.../node_modules/openclaw` (exit 0).
- The real prefix's `node_modules/openclaw/package.json` still reads `2026.6.11` after the "successful" install+swap steps.

(Interesting detail visible in a single `update --json` document: fields derived from `process.execPath` show the real UUID path, fields derived from npm stdout show the `***` path — a clean fingerprint of where the poisoning enters.)

## Trigger surface

Any environment where the npm global prefix path contains a UUID-shaped segment (case-insensitive `8-4-4-4-12` hex): ephemeral CI workdirs, per-session sandboxes, tmp-scoped toolchains. npm redacts stdout of at least `npm root -g` and `npm prefix -g`.

## Suggested fixes (any of these breaks the chain)

1. Validate the value returned by `resolveGlobalRoot`: reject it (fall back) if the path does not exist, or if it contains npm's redaction marker `***`.
2. Prefer deriving the global root from the running package root (`pkgRoot`, process-derived, never redacted) in the `update` flow — e.g. pass `honorPackageRoot: true` at `update-runner.ts` call site, or use `inferGlobalRootFromPackageRoot` before the command probe rather than after it fails.
3. Query the prefix in a redaction-free way (e.g. `node -p "process.execPath"`-relative resolution) instead of parsing npm's decorated stdout.

Note the same `stdout.trim()` pattern is also used by `detectGlobalInstallManagerForRoot` (v2026.6.11 L814–835) — there the poisoned path fails realpath comparison against the real pkgRoot, which can additionally cause **manager misdetection** (npm probe silently fails to match its own root).

*(Found while investigating #106920 in a sandbox; investigation and repro scripting were AI-assisted.)*

title: `openclaw update` trusts `npm root -g` stdout verbatim, but npm >= 11 redacts UUID-like path segments to literal `***` — update then installs into a literal `***/` directory tree and silently leaves the live install stale
author: SL4N
body:
## Summary

`resolveGlobalRoot()` shells out to `npm root -g` and uses `res.stdout.trim()` as a real filesystem path. Since npm added `@npmcli/redact`, **all** npm output — including plain stdout of `npm root -g` / `npm prefix -g` — is passed through `redactLog`, which replaces every UUID-shaped substring (`[0-9a-f]{8}-[0-9a-f]{4}-...`) with the literal string `***`.

If the npm global prefix path contains a UUID-like segment (common for per-session sandboxes, ephemeral CI workspaces, tmpdir-scoped installs), `npm root -g` returns a **poisoned path** such as:

```
/tmp/ci-agent/***/workspace/sbx-***/nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/lib/node_modules
```

The updater then:

1. `fs.mkdir(globalRoot, { recursive: true })` — **creates a literal `***` directory tree on disk** (`createStagedNpmInstall`),
2. runs `npm i -g --prefix <***-path>/.openclaw-update-stage-XXXXXX <spec>` — installs the new version into the phantom tree,
3. swaps the staged package into `<***-path>/node_modules/openclaw`,
4. reports the update as installed, while the **real** global install is left untouched at the old version.

Net effect: `openclaw update` is a silent no-op for the live install, plus a junk parallel directory tree literally named `***`.

## Affected code (verified at v2026.6.11; still present on current `main`, commit `1b9aca26b`)

- `src/infra/update-global.ts` v2026.6.11 L737–743 (main L751–757): `resolveGlobalRoot` → `const root = res.stdout.trim(); return root || null;` — trusts redacted stdout.
- `src/infra/update-runner.ts` v2026.6.11 L1661: `resolveGlobalInstallTarget({ manager, runCommand, timeoutMs, pkgRoot })` — called **without** `honorPackageRoot`, so the (real, process-derived) `pkgRoot` never overrides the poisoned stdout value; `targetGlobalRoot` falls through to `globalRoot` (update-global.ts v2026.6.11 L782–786).
- `src/infra/package-update-steps.ts` v2026.6.11 L248–249: `createStagedNpmInstall` → `fs.mkdir(targetLayout.globalRoot, { recursive: true })` + `mkdtemp` — materializes the literal `***` tree; L499 (swap step) and L621 (`stagedInstall?.prefix` → `--prefix`) propagate it.
- npm side (npm 11.9.0, node 24.14.0): `node_modules/npm/lib/utils/format.js` — comment "All logging goes through here, both to console and log files" → `redactLog` from `@npmcli/redact` (`lib/index.js`: `const REPLACE = '***'` + `matchers.UUID.pattern`).

## Reproduction (fully scripted, no manual path handling)

Self-contained script (creates a UUID-named sandbox prefix, installs openclaw@2026.6.11, runs `openclaw update --yes --no-restart --json`, then does byte-level forensics via `os.listdir`/file reads — no shell display involved):

Observed on Linux arm64, node v24.14.0, npm 11.9.0:

- Mechanism probe (bytes captured via `subprocess`, not a terminal):
  `npm root -g` → `b'/tmp/ci-agent/***/workspace/sbx-***/nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/lib/node_modules\n'`
- Baseline: no `***` path exists anywhere.
- After `openclaw update`: a literal `***` tree exists (verified with byte-level `os.listdir`, entry `b'***'`), containing a freshly installed `openclaw@2026.7.1`; `update --json` step log shows
  `npm i -g --prefix /tmp/.../***/.../.openclaw-update-stage-AkrV8k openclaw@latest` (exit 0) and
  `swap .../***/.../.openclaw-update-stage-AkrV8k/lib/node_modules/openclaw -> .../***/.../node_modules/openclaw` (exit 0).
- The real prefix's `node_modules/openclaw/package.json` still reads `2026.6.11` after the "successful" install+swap steps.

(Interesting detail visible in a single `update --json` document: fields derived from `process.execPath` show the real UUID path, fields derived from npm stdout show the `***` path — a clean fingerprint of where the poisoning enters.)

## Trigger surface

Any environment where the npm global prefix path contains a UUID-shaped segment (case-insensitive `8-4-4-4-12` hex): ephemeral CI workdirs, per-session sandboxes, tmp-scoped toolchains. npm redacts stdout of at least `npm root -g` and `npm prefix -g`.

## Suggested fixes (any of these breaks the chain)

1. Validate the value returned by `resolveGlobalRoot`: reject it (fall back) if the path does not exist, or if it contains npm's redaction marker `***`.
2. Prefer deriving the global root from the running package root (`pkgRoot`, process-derived, never redacted) in the `update` flow — e.g. pass `honorPackageRoot: true` at `update-runner.ts` call site, or use `inferGlobalRootFromPackageRoot` before the command probe rather than after it fails.
3. Query the prefix in a redaction-free way (e.g. `node -p "process.execPath"`-relative resolution) instead of parsing npm's decorated stdout.

Note the same `stdout.trim()` pattern is also used by `detectGlobalInstallManagerForRoot` (v2026.6.11 L814–835) — there the poisoned path fails realpath comparison against the real pkgRoot, which can additionally cause **manager misdetection** (npm probe silently fails to match its own root).

*(Found while investigating #106920 in a sandbox; investigation and repro scripting were AI-assisted.)*

title: [Feature]: Two-tier agent capabilities: session self-service tools + OpenClaw system-agent delegation (Crestodian rename)
author: steipete
body:
### Summary

Give agents parity with the Control UI in two tiers: direct session-level self-service tools, and system-level configuration delegated to the ring-zero system agent — renamed from Crestodian to **OpenClaw** ("talk to openclaw, openclaw is the system").

### Problem to solve

A capability audit (Control UI RPC map vs. agent tool inventory vs. gateway method registry) shows the web app drives ~200 gateway methods while agents are missing everyday session operations and have no sanctioned path for system configuration:

- Agents cannot rename/label/pin/archive their own session, manage session groups, or switch their own session model (`sessions.patch`, `sessions.groups.*` have no tool).
- Agents cannot create a user-visible session, and `sessions_spawn` never provisions worktrees — even though `sessions.create` already supports `worktree/worktreeName/worktreeBaseRef` and the task-suggestion accept path proves the pattern (`src/gateway/server-methods/task-suggestions.ts` → `sessions.create { worktree: true }`).
- Agents have no unified background-work view. The `task_runs` ledger already unifies subagent/acp/media-generation/cron runs and the UI Tasks page is a view over it, but the `subagents` tool reads a narrower registry (own subagent runs only, no media/cron, no cancel-by-task).
- No cost/usage visibility (`usage.cost`, `sessions.usage` are UI-only).
- System configuration is either impossible for agents (channels, plugins, agent creation, model/provider auth) or — worse — available as direct writes via the `gateway` tool (`config.apply/patch`, `update.run`, restart), bypassing any human-approval boundary.

Meanwhile Crestodian already has exactly the right safety machinery for tier 2: a three-gate approval flow (model claims approval, host independently verifies the human's message, operation-hash match) with host-side post-turn apply and audit (`src/agents/tools/crestodian-tool.ts`, `src/crestodian/chat-engine.ts`). It just has no agent-facing seam, and the name doesn't communicate "this is the system."

### Proposed solution

Two-tier model: **agents self-serve their own sessions; agents ask the OpenClaw system agent for anything system-level** (config, channels, plugins, new agents, model/provider changes, updates). Humans stay the only approvers of persistent writes.

Phased, one stacked PR per phase:

**Phase 0 — rename Crestodian → OpenClaw**
- Agent-facing tool `openclaw`; gateway methods `openclaw.chat` / `openclaw.setup.*`; reserved agent id `openclaw`; persona "OpenClaw" (the system).
- CLI: `openclaw setup` becomes the system-agent entry on a configured interactive system; unconfigured systems and all existing wizard/onboard flags fall through to onboarding unchanged (additive to the public CLI contract). Hidden `crestodian` alias kept; docs redirect.
- macOS app onboarding/Settings strings updated in lockstep (in-repo client, atomic protocol rename).

**Phase 1 — session self-service (direct tools, write-scope only)**
- Grouped `sessions` management tool: `patch` (label/pin/archive/model/thinking) + `groups` (list/put/rename/delete). No reset/delete/compact — not in the action enum.
- `sessions_spawn` gains `visible: true` + `worktree` (+ name/baseRef). Visible path routes through `sessions.create` (dashboard key, user-visible), keeps the completion-announce contract (system notification the parent may ignore), visibility stays tree-scoped like subagents. No `cwd` param (admin-scope escalation stays closed); worktree defaults to the agent workspace repo.
- `subagents` tool reads the task ledger (same filter as the Tasks page): media-generation/acp/cron runs visible, plus cancel.
- `session_status` gains a cost/tokens line from the session-cost cache.
- Model self-patch safety: when the agent's own patch changed the session model and the next run fails on model/auth error, auto-revert to the last-working model with a system note (agent cannot nerf itself; user-initiated switches untouched).

**Phase 2 — OpenClaw delegation**
- New `openclaw` agent tool relaying to `openclaw.chat` in-process. Persistent writes surface as approval cards through the existing `approval.get/resolve` operator overlay — only a human resolve arms the apply; the write/audit/post-turn machinery is unchanged.
- Vendor-neutral inference fallback ladder so the system agent always works: agent's configured model first, then other authed providers; failover on auth/availability errors only.
- System prompt (token-lean register) steers agents: config/channels/plugins/agents/updates → ask `openclaw`; never write own config. Plus the `visible` rule keyed off the turn source channel (webchat/app → visible spawn usually right; messaging channels → hidden unless asked).
- `update.run` moves behind this path.

**Phase 3 — narrow the `gateway` tool**
- Keep `config.get`/schema reads. Remove writes/`update.run`/restart from the default toolset; operators who want direct writes re-enable via the existing per-agent tool allowlist — no new config key.
- Docs pass for all phases.

### Alternatives considered

- Flat direct tools for everything (plugins.install, agents.create, config writes as agent tools): rejected — no human approval hop; agents self-modifying system config is the failure mode this design exists to prevent.
- Separate `tasks` agent tool: rejected — `task_runs` is already the unified ledger; extending the `subagents` view avoids a second listing concept.
- New "agent" gateway connection role: unnecessary — the gateway has operator/node roles only, and in-process dispatch with least-privilege operator scopes already carries agent identity.
- Keeping the Crestodian name with better docs: users don't get it; "openclaw" self-describes as the system.

### Impact

- Affected: every agent operator surface (webchat, macOS app, channels), all agents.
- Severity: capability gap — common asks ("archive this session", "start a visible session in a worktree for this", "what's running?", "set up Telegram") are impossible or unsafe today.
- Frequency: daily operations.
- Consequence: users context-switch to the Control UI for actions the agent should handle; direct `gateway` writes remain an unmediated risk surface.

### Evidence/examples

- Approval machinery: `src/agents/tools/crestodian-tool.ts` (three-gate approval, `catalogMode: direct-only`), `src/crestodian/chat-engine.ts` (host post-turn apply, live inference re-check), `src/crestodian/operations.ts` (persistent-op boundary + audit).
- Session/worktree precedent: `src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.ts` (`sessions.create` worktree provisioning, owner-bound GC), `src/gateway/server-methods/task-suggestions.ts` (agent-triggered visible worktree session).
- Spawn divergence: `src/agents/subagent-spawn.ts` (hand-rolled `subagent:` child, no worktree path).
- Unified ledger: `src/tasks/task-registry.store.sqlite.ts` (`task_runs` across subagent/acp/cli-media/cron), `src/gateway/server-methods/tasks.ts`, UI views `ui/src/pages/tasks/`, `ui/src/lib/workboard/task-links.ts`.
- Scope model: `src/gateway/methods/core-descriptors.ts`, `src/gateway/method-scopes.ts` (`sessions.create` dynamic admin escalation on `cwd`/`execNode` — deliberately not exposed to agents).

### Additional information

- Prompt and tool text in the token-lean telegraph register.
- Turn source (`messageChannel`/`turnSourceChannel`) already reaches the run context; the `visible` guidance needs prompt text only, no new plumbing.
- Compatibility: hidden `crestodian` CLI alias, docs redirect, onboarding flags preserved; no other legacy shims.

title: 2026.7.1 plugin loader resolves inconsistently between flat project dirs and new `__openclaw-generation__` dirs — a `plugins install`/sync of a new version can silently not take effect
author: Marvinthebored
body:
# 2026.7.1 plugin loader resolves inconsistently between flat project dirs and new `__openclaw-generation__` dirs — a `plugins install`/sync of a new version can silently not take effect

> **Honest caveat up front:** I observed this **during and after a failed-then-manually-repaired state migration** (see companion issue about the migration gate). I have **not** reproduced it on a clean 2026.7.1 install, so it may be a *symptom* of the incomplete migration rather than an independent loader bug. Filing it because the observed behavior — an installed new plugin version silently not loading — is severe if it's real, and because I don't understand the intended selection logic. Please treat the "is this a bug or expected?" question as the first thing to answer.

## Summary

On this `2026.7.1` install, `openclaw plugins list` shows plugins loading from **two different location schemes at once**:

- some from the **flat** project dir: `~/.openclaw/npm/projects/<name>-<hash>/node_modules/@openclaw/<pkg>`
- some from a new **generation** dir: `~/.openclaw/npm/projects/<name>-<hash>__openclaw-generation__g-<hash>/node_modules/@openclaw/<pkg>`

Concretely, on the same install:

| Plugin | Loaded from | Version loaded |
|---|---|---|
| `deepseek`, `firecrawl`, `perplexity` (providers) | **generation** dir | 2026.6.11 |
| `clickclack` | **flat** dir | 2026.6.11 (stale — see below) |
| `codex` (freshly installed) | **flat** dir | 2026.7.1 |

The damaging consequence: **`openclaw update`'s plugin sync installed `@openclaw/clickclack@2026.7.1` into a `__openclaw-generation__` dir, but the loader kept using the flat dir at `2026.6.11`.** The update reported the plugin installed; `plugins list` showed it enabled; but the *loaded* code was the old version. It only took effect after I manually copied the 2026.7.1 content into the flat dir.

## Environment

- OpenClaw **2026.7.1** (`2d2ddc4`), upgraded from **2026.6.11** via `openclaw update` (npm, stable), macOS, Node 25.9.0, launchd gateway.
- **Long-lived install**, and — importantly — the migration into the new shared-SQLite plugin index **did not complete cleanly** and was repaired by hand (moving legacy `installs.json`/`update-check.json` aside). The mixed flat/generation resolution was observed against that repaired-but-possibly-inconsistent state.

## Observed

1. `openclaw update` → "Installing @openclaw/clickclack@2026.7.1 into …__openclaw-generation__g-…".
2. Gateway restart → `openclaw plugins list` shows `clickclack` **enabled**, but the "location" column points at the **flat** dir, and its version is **2026.6.11** (the pre-upgrade version).
3. Meanwhile `deepseek`/`firecrawl`/`perplexity` load from their **generation** dirs.
4. So the install target (generation dir) and the load source (flat dir) disagree for some plugins → a "successful" plugin install/update silently has no effect.

## Questions for maintainers (before treating as a confirmed bug)

- In 2026.7.1, which is the **canonical** load source — the flat project dir or the `__openclaw-generation__` dir?
- Is generation→loaded "promotion" gated on the state migration completing cleanly? (If so, this is likely a symptom of the migration issue, and the fix belongs there.)
- If both schemes are expected to coexist, what determines which one wins per plugin, and should `plugins install`/`update` guarantee the newly-installed version is the one that loads?

## Expected

`openclaw plugins install` / the `openclaw update` plugin sync should guarantee that the version they install is the version the loader actually loads — never leave a newer install in a directory the loader ignores while continuing to run an older copy.

## Workaround (what I did)

Manually mirror the intended (new) plugin version into the dir the loader actually reads (the flat dir), then restart. Obviously not something a normal user would find.

## Impact if real

A routine `plugins update` / `openclaw update` can silently no-op — the user believes a plugin is updated (install succeeded, `plugins list` shows the new version "installed") while the gateway keeps executing the old code. Hard to notice, easy to misattribute.

title: 2026.7.1 startup-migration gate is fatal, but the repair path (`openclaw doctor`) doesn't resolve the conflict — gateway crash-loops with no documented remedy
author: Marvinthebored
body:
# 2026.7.1 startup-migration gate is fatal, but the repair path (`openclaw doctor`) doesn't resolve the conflict — gateway crash-loops with no documented remedy

## Summary

Upgrading a long-lived install to `2026.7.1` left the gateway **refusing to start**, crash-looping under launchd, with:

```
[openclaw] Reason: OpenClaw startup migrations did not complete cleanly; refusing to report the gateway ready.
- Left plugin install index in place because shared SQLite state has conflicting plugin install metadata for: deepseek, firecrawl, perplexity, qwen
- Left legacy update-check state in place because shared SQLite state already differs: /Users/.../.openclaw/update-check.json
- Left legacy config health state in place because 1 entry conflicts with shared SQLite state: /Users/.../.openclaw/logs/config-health.json
[openclaw] Try: openclaw doctor
```

The error directs the user to `openclaw doctor`, **but `doctor --fix` does not resolve these conflicts** — it reconciles other state (auth-profile SQLite, config) and leaves the plugin-install-index / legacy-state conflicts exactly as-is, so the gateway keeps failing the gate. There is no documented or automated way to complete the migration; the only fix I found was **manually moving the conflicting legacy JSON files aside**.

`2026.7.1` intentionally makes an unclean migration fatal ("gateway crashes stop restarting forever / stable repair path"). The problem isn't the gate — it's that **the advertised repair path doesn't repair**, and three secondary issues make recovery worse (below).

## Environment

- OpenClaw **2026.7.1** (`2d2ddc4`), upgraded from **2026.6.11** via `openclaw update` (npm, stable).
- macOS (Darwin 24.6.0), Node 25.9.0, npm-global, gateway under launchd (`KeepAlive`).
- **Long-lived install**: the state dir has `~/.openclaw/plugins/installs.json`, `~/.openclaw/update-check.json`, `~/.openclaw/logs/config-health.json` accumulated across many prior versions — these predate the new shared-SQLite store and conflict with the entries the migration wrote into it.

## The core bug

1. Startup migration finds legacy JSON state that **conflicts** with the new shared-SQLite store → it "leaves the legacy state in place" and marks the migration incomplete.
2. The incomplete migration trips the **fatal readiness gate** → gateway never reports ready → launchd respawns it every ~13s (crash loop).
3. The error says **"Try: openclaw doctor"**, but `openclaw doctor --fix` **does not reconcile** the plugin-install-index or legacy-state conflicts — they persist verbatim across repeated `doctor --fix` runs.

**Expected:** `doctor --fix` (or `openclaw update repair`) should resolve legacy-vs-SQLite conflicts — e.g. adopt one side deterministically, or offer a `--force-adopt-sqlite` / `--reset-legacy-state` path — **or** the error should name the exact files to move and the command to run. Right now the user is told to run a tool that can't fix it, with no next step.

## Three secondary issues that make recovery worse

- **b1. Stale migration lease-lock under crash-loop.** Once looping, starts begin failing with a *different* error:
  ```
  OpenClaw startup migrations are already running for this state directory; retry after <timestamp>.
  ```
  The launchd respawns contend the migration lease and effectively keep it held, so it never clears within the respawn cycle. Recovery requires fully unloading the service (`launchctl bootout`) and waiting out the lease before a single clean start.
- **b2. `doctor --fix` restarts the crash-looping gateway mid-repair.** Running `doctor --fix` to repair the loop **re-bootstraps/restarts the LaunchAgent**, re-arming the crash loop and lease contention while you're trying to diagnose. A repair command shouldn't restart the unhealthy service without first resolving the cause.
- **b3. "Stable repair path" still thrashes.** The release notes describe repeatedly-failing gateways leaving "a stable repair path instead of restarting indefinitely," but in practice launchd `KeepAlive` respawned the failing process every ~13s indefinitely until manually booted out.

## Workaround

```bash
launchctl bootout gui/$UID/ai.openclaw.gateway         # stop the crash loop
# move the conflicting legacy state aside (regenerable caches):
mv ~/.openclaw/plugins/installs.json      ~/.openclaw/plugins/installs.json.bak
mv ~/.openclaw/update-check.json          ~/.openclaw/update-check.json.bak
mv ~/.openclaw/logs/config-health.json    ~/.openclaw/logs/config-health.json.bak   # if present
# wait out the migration lease, then ONE clean start:
openclaw gateway restart
```

After this the migration completes against the SQLite store and the gateway comes up.

## Impact / notes

The conflicting legacy files come from a long-lived install, which most users won't have — so the *trigger* is niche. But the failure mode (fatal gate + a repair path that can't repair + a crash loop that holds its own lease) is a rough first-run experience for exactly the upgrade-from-old-version case the migration exists to serve. Making `doctor --fix` actually reconcile these, or naming the files in the error, would resolve it.

title: `doctor --fix` silently drops the `plugins.allow` entry for a bundled→externalized plugin (codex), unregistering the agent harness — and the gateway still reports healthy
author: Marvinthebored
body:
# `doctor --fix` silently drops the `plugins.allow` entry for a bundled→externalized plugin (codex), unregistering the agent harness — and the gateway still reports healthy

## Summary

After upgrading `2026.6.11 → 2026.7.1`, the **codex agent harness stopped being registered**, so any agent bound to it fails every turn with:

```
GatewayClientRequestError: MissingAgentHarnessError: Requested agent harness "codex" is not registered.
```

Root cause: `2026.7.1` **externalizes the codex harness** into the `@openclaw/codex` plugin (previously effectively bundled/allowed as `codex`). During the upgrade, `doctor`/`doctor --fix` **silently removed `"codex"` from `plugins.allow`** because no installed plugin resolved to that id. With the fail-closed allowlist, the harness never loaded.

There are really **two problems** here:

1. **Silent allowlist strip.** A config-reconciliation step deleted a user's `plugins.allow` entry for a capability that was *externalized*, rather than migrating it (installing the replacement) or surfacing a blocking warning. The upgrade turned a working, configured capability into a silent removal.
2. **Health check is green while a configured harness is missing.** The gateway reported `running` / healthy, the port was open, `plugins list` looked fine, and there were **zero error log lines** — yet no agent using the codex harness could run. Readiness/health does not account for "a configured agent's required harness failed to register." This is the more broadly-important issue: nothing short of an actual agent turn revealed the breakage.

## Environment

- OpenClaw **2026.7.1** (`2d2ddc4`), upgraded from **2026.6.11** (`b2355ef`) via `openclaw update` (npm install, `stable` channel).
- macOS (Darwin 24.6.0), Node 25.9.0, npm-global install at `/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw`, gateway under a launchd LaunchAgent.
- Long-lived install (agents were bound to the `codex` harness for many prior versions).

## Reproduction

1. On a pre-7.1 install with an agent using the codex harness and `"codex"` present in `plugins.allow`, upgrade to `2026.7.1` via `openclaw update`.
2. Observe the gateway comes up "healthy" (port open, `openclaw status` shows running, no error lines).
3. Run a real turn against the agent: `openclaw agent --agent <main> --session-id smoke -m "ping"`.
4. It fails with `MissingAgentHarnessError: Requested agent harness "codex" is not registered.`

## Observed vs expected

**Observed**
- `plugins.allow` diff across the upgrade: `"codex"` present before, **removed after** (it was the only allowlist delta).
- `plugins list` even prints the hint (so the tooling *knows* what happened):
  ```
  - plugins.allow: plugin not installed: codex — install the official external plugin with: openclaw plugins install @openclaw/codex
  ```
  …but this is a soft config-warning, not surfaced as a blocking/health-affecting condition, and the allow entry was already deleted.

**Expected** (any of):
- When a bundled plugin is externalized, the upgrade **migrates** it — installs `@openclaw/codex` (pinned to the host cohort) and keeps the allow entry — rather than deleting the entry.
- If it can't auto-install, it **fails/warns loudly** (non-zero doctor status, prominent banner) instead of silently pruning `plugins.allow`.
- Gateway **readiness/health reflects missing harnesses**: if a configured agent's harness isn't registered, health should be degraded/unhealthy, not green.

## Workaround

```bash
# restore the allowlist entry
openclaw config set plugins.allow '[...,"codex",...]' --strict-json
# install the now-external plugin, pinned to host version to avoid skew
openclaw plugins install '@openclaw/codex@2026.7.1' --force --pin
openclaw gateway restart
# verify with an ACTUAL agent turn, not just health
openclaw agent --agent main --session-id smoke -m "ping"
```

## Impact

Silent, total loss of any agent on the externalized harness after a routine `openclaw update`, with a green health check masking it. Easy to ship "successfully" and only discover when a user's agent won't answer.
